title: Protocol: Cross-sectional registry analysis of traditional medicine trial density in Africa versus global comparators
body: This cross-sectional registry study will quantify the gap between Africa's reliance on traditional medicine and the registered evidence base on ClinicalTrials.gov. We will query ClinicalTrials.gov API v2 for interventional studies matching traditional medicine, herbal, medicinal plant, or indigenous medicine across eight African countries, China, India, Brazil, and the United States, plus seven African-origin remedies. The primary outcome is the ratio of Chinese to African traditional medicine trial counts benchmarked against the WHO estimate that 80% of Africans rely on traditional medicine. Secondary analyses will examine per-country breakdowns, remedy-specific availability, sponsor class distribution, and compliance with the WHO Traditional Medicine Strategy 2014 to 2023. All queries will be scripted in Python, cached with 24-hour time-to-live, and archived with the HTML dashboard for reproducibility. No patient-level data will be accessed and no ethics approval is required for aggregate registry queries. Limitations include restriction to one English-language registry and inability to assess trial quality or completeness from metadata alone.

sentences.text: This cross-sectional registry study will quantify the gap between Africa's population-level reliance on traditional medicine and the registered evidence base for herbal and indigenous remedies on ClinicalTrials.gov.

We will query ClinicalTrials.gov API v2 for all interventional studies matching traditional medicine, herbal, medicinal plant, or indigenous medicine across eight African countries, China, India, Brazil, and the United States, plus seven African-origin remedies.
The primary outcome is the ratio of Chinese to African traditional medicine trial counts, benchmarked against the WHO estimate that 80% of Africans use traditional medicine.
Secondary analyses will examine per-country breakdowns, remedy-specific trial availability, sponsor class distribution, and compliance with the WHO Traditional Medicine Strategy 2014 to 2023.
All queries will be scripted in Python, cached with 24-hour time-to-live, and archived alongside the generated HTML dashboard for full reproducibility.
No patient-level data will be accessed and no ethics approval is required for registry-level aggregate queries.
Limitations include restriction to a single English-language registry, possible undercounting on WHO or national platforms, and inability to assess trial quality from registry metadata alone.

outsideNote.data: ClinicalTrials.gov API v2, 8 African countries, 5 comparison regions, 7 specific remedies

ai_transparency: LLM assistance was used for drafting and language editing. The author reviewed and edited the manuscript and takes responsibility for the final content.
